feed.title: Karthik V. Sarma | Notes
feed.subtitle: Physician-scientist, technology executive, and policy advisor working at the intersection of artificial intelligence, mental health, and the clinic.

entry.title: On “AI psychosis” as a category error

link.title: On “AI psychosis” as a category error

entry.content:
Over the past eighteen months, the term “AI psychosis” has migrated from a handful of case reports into the popular press, and from there into the way some clinicians have started to describe patients to me. I want to push back on this. The phrase compresses three things that we should be keeping separate: an exposure (sustained interaction with a particular kind of conversational AI), a clinical phenomenon (delusional or quasi-delusional content that references the AI), and a mechanism (whatever is going on that links the two).

Each of these deserves its own scrutiny, but collapsing them into a single named entity does the opposite. It implies a uniform syndrome, a uniform mechanism, and — most consequentially — a uniform clinical response. None of these are warranted by what we currently know.

What we actually see

The clinical picture across the case reports varies considerably. In some, a patient with a clear premorbid history of psychotic illness experiences an acute decompensation in which content referencing the AI features prominently. In others, the patient has no such history, the presentation is acute, and the content is florid but resolves rapidly with brief abstinence from the AI. In still others, what is being labeled psychosis looks closer to a fixed and unusual but non-bizarre belief that doesn’t generalize beyond the conversational frame.

These are three quite different things, and conflating them under one heading invites three different kinds of clinical error: under-treating the first by attributing it primarily to the AI exposure, over-treating the second by reaching for the antipsychotic when supportive care and abstinence would do, and pathologizing the third when the more parsimonious read is that we are watching the limits of a particular consumer product surface in a measurable way.

What I’m proposing

Treat “AI psychosis” as a journalistic descriptor, not a clinical one. In clinical documentation, write what you see — the exposure history, the actual symptoms, the mental status — and let the diagnostic framework do the rest. If we find ourselves repeatedly reaching for a single phrase to describe what we are seeing, that is a sign that we need finer distinctions, not a more efficient label.

More on this, including what colleagues at the Stanford Brainstorm group and our team at UCSF are working on to measure these interactions properly, in a forthcoming paper.
entry.summary: The label has stuck because it is useful shorthand for journalists. It is not useful for clinicians, and treating it as a diagnostic category — rather than a description of a presentation — leads us in the wrong direction.

entry.title: What model validation should actually require

link.title: What model validation should actually require

entry.content:
Benchmark performance and deployment safety are different claims, and the distance between them is where most of the risk lives. Here are three things I keep asking for that aren’t yet standard.

1. Failure-mode characterization, not just aggregate accuracy

An aggregate accuracy number tells you how often the model is right on average. It tells you nothing about how it fails — and in a clinical setting, the shape of the failure matters more than its frequency.

2. Behavior under sustained, adversarial, multi-turn interaction

Most evaluations are single-turn. The cases that worry me clinically are not.

3. A monitoring plan that survives contact with production

A model that was validated once, at a point in time, on a fixed distribution, is not the model that will be running in six months.
entry.summary: A note on the gap between 'the model achieves SOTA on this benchmark' and 'this model is safe to deploy in front of a patient.' Three things I keep asking companies for that aren't standard yet.

entry.title: Reading: the GPT-5 system card, three weeks in

link.title: Reading: the GPT-5 system card, three weeks in

entry.content:
Three weeks of reading and re-reading the card, with a clinical-deployment lens. A few observations.

What’s genuinely useful

The expanded refusal taxonomy is the most clinically relevant addition, and it is better than I expected.

What’s missing

There is still very little about sustained multi-session interaction, which is exactly the regime where the cases I see clinically arise.

What the stress tests actually tell us

Stress-test pass rates are reassuring at the margin and uninformative at the center.
entry.summary: What's in the card that matters for clinical applications, what's missing, and what the model's behavior under stress tests actually tells us about deployment risk.

entry.title: Five questions I now ask every digital-health founder

link.title: Five questions I now ask every digital-health founder

entry.content:
After a year of advisory conversations, these five questions do most of the work of telling me whether a team has thought seriously about clinical safety.

  1. What is the worst thing your product can do to a user, and how would you find out it happened?
  2. Who on your team can overrule the growth metric?
  3. What is your plan for the user who is in crisis right now?
  4. How do you know your model hasn’t drifted since you last validated it?
  5. What would make you pull the product?

The companies that have good answers are not always the ones with the best technology. But they are the ones I worry about least.
entry.summary: After a year of advisory conversations, a short list of the questions that separate companies that have thought about clinical safety from companies that haven't yet.

entry.title: Why most chatbot mental-health studies miss

link.title: Why most chatbot mental-health studies miss

entry.content:
There is a structural mismatch between the phenomenon and the method. The cases that reach a clinic involve transcripts that run to hundreds of pages over days. The studies that inform policy involve a handful of turns in a controlled setting.

The mismatch

A single-turn vignette can establish that a model can produce a harmful response. It cannot establish how a vulnerable person and a model co-evolve a belief over seventy-two hours.

Why it’s hard to fix

Simulating sustained interaction is genuinely difficult, and the obvious shortcuts introduce their own artifacts. This is the methodological problem our group is most focused on right now.
entry.summary: The cases we see clinically involve sustained, multi-day, multi-turn conversations. The studies we have are mostly single-turn vignettes. The gap matters, and it is harder to close than it looks.

entry.title: Notes from an AMA delegate, on AI policy

link.title: Notes from an AMA delegate, on AI policy

entry.content:
A view from inside the process, written for physicians who have never seen how organized medicine actually makes policy on AI.

What the House actually does

Policy here is slower, more deliberative, and more consequential than most clinicians assume.

What the Informatics Caucus is working on

A short tour of the live questions, without the inside baseball.

The intent-to-practice gap

The distance between a well-crafted resolution and a changed clinical workflow is the part nobody owns, and it is where most of the real work remains.
entry.summary: What the AMA House of Delegates actually does on AI, what the Informatics Caucus is currently working on, and why the gap between policy intent and clinical practice is wider than most physicians realize.
